steps.title: Read the frequency distributions from both dot plots
steps.content:
From the dot plot for Class A: reading left to right at positions 1 through 7, the frequency (height) of dots above each position is 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 5.

From the dot plot for Class B: reading left to right at positions 14 through 20, the frequency of dots above each position is 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 5.

steps.title: Recognize that both distributions have identical frequency patterns
steps.content: Both dot plots show exactly the same shape: the frequencies increase from 1 to 6, then decrease back to 5. The only difference is the location on the number line — Class A centers near the lower numbers (1–7), while Class B centers near the higher numbers (14–20). The pattern itself is identical.

steps.title: Apply the shift property of standard deviation
steps.content:
Standard deviation measures how spread out values are from their mean. When all values in a dataset are shifted by adding the same constant (here, each value increases by 13: from the 1–7 range to the 14–20 range), the mean shifts by that same amount, but the distances of each value from its mean remain exactly the same.

Mathematically, if a dataset $\{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}$ has standard deviation $\sigma$, then the shifted dataset $\{x_1 + c, x_2 + c, \ldots, x_n + c\}$ has the same standard deviation $\sigma$.

steps.title: Conclude with the answer
steps.content: Because Class A and Class B have identical frequency distributions (same shape), just with values shifted by a constant, they must have the same standard deviation. That matches choice B — B is correct.
